rulings.Case Title: RAY BRONNER VS. RICHARD S KAIHARA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Consolidated Discovery Motion For Order That The Truth Of Any Matters In Request For Admissions Are Deemed Admitted
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, DECEMBER 21, 2012, LINE 1, DEFENDANT RICHARD KAIHARA, JEFF LANCE, HAYMAN BROTHERS, INC. Notice Of Consolidated Discovery Motion For Order That The Truth Of Any Matters In Request For Admissions Are Deemed Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted. Plaintiff shall provide responses, without objections or assertions of privilege, as to Defendants' first sets of form interrogatories, special interrogatories, and request for production of documents, as well as Defendants' second set of form interrogatories, within 20 days of the date of this order. The truth of the matters specified in Defendants' Requests for Admissions, Set One, is deemed admitted, and any objections thereto are waived. (Code Civ. Proc., §2033.280, subd. (a), (b).) Plaintiff shall pay sanctions of $640.00 (4 hours at $145/hr plus $60 filing fee) to Defendants' counsel within 20 days of the date of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: AL EBNETER et al VS. LUKE BRUGNARA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Withdraw Admitted Requests For Admissions And Allow Deft. To Serve Discovery Responses And Supporting Memorandum Of P & A
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, DECEMBER 21, 2012, LINE 3, DEFENDANT LUKE BRUGNARA Motion To Withdraw Admitted Requests For Admissions And Allow Defendant To Serve Discovery Responses. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to withdraw requests deemed admitted is granted. The Court's order of March 20, 2012 specifically permitted Defendant to bring this motion. Mr. Brugnara's incarceration in solitary confinement and the failure of federal authorities to provide access to his mail or to phone contact with his counsel constitutes excusable neglect sufficient to permit the granting of relief. Any claimed prejudice to Plaintiffs is outweighed by the interest in having claims adjudicated on their merits. (New Albertson's, Inc. v. Superior Court (2009) 168 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1420.) Plaintiffs may pursue additional discovery related to the matters involved in the withdrawn admissions, and Defendant is ordered to bear the cost of such additional discovery in an amount to be determined by the Court upon subsequent motion by Plaintiffs after the conclusion of such discovery. (Code Civ. Proc., §2033.300, subd. (c).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SAEED J IBRAHIM VS. EXTREME AUTO RECOVERY COMPANY
rulings.Calendar Matter: AMENDED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 21, 2012, Line 4, DEFENDANT EXTREME AUTO RECOVERY, INC.'S AMENDED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The Court grants summary judgment. Plaintiff cannot establish the causes of action for false imprisonment, assault and battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress, negligent infliction of emotional distress, and violation of Unruh Act because the claims are based upon Mr. Jones' allegedly false report to the police. Mr. Jones' report to the police is absolutely privileged under Civil Procedure Code Section 47(b), and cannot be the basis for the torts alleged in this case. Hagberg v. Cal. Fed Bank FSB (2004) 32 Cal.4th 350, 364. Further, Defendant is entitled to summary adjudication on the assault and battery cause of action because plaintiff admits that Mr. Jones did not engage in such conduct and Plaintiff cites no authority holding that defendant can be liable for police misconduct. Defendant is entitled to summary adjudication on the negligent hiring cause of action because there is no proof that Mr. Jones committed an actionable underlying tort. The request to deny the motion for summary judgment pursuant to CCP §437c(i) or to order the Defendant to produce Mr. Jones for deposition or other discovery is denied. The Court assumes that Plaintiff was referring to CCP §437c(h). Plaintiff has not made the required showing that facts essential to justify opposition may exist, but could not be presented in opposition to the motion regarding the cause of action for negligent hiring, retention and supervision. In fact, Plaintiff's declaration suggests that Mr. Jones would not be available to be a witness at all. (Ibrahim Decl. 9.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: MECHANICS BANK, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. JOHN T. KONTRABECKI et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn Tfor Protective Orders
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, DECEMBER 21, 2012, LINE 4, DEFENDANT JOHN KONTRABECKI, NICOLE KONTRABECKI Motion for Protective Orders. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is denied. The privilege not to testify against a spouse (Evid. Code, §970) does not apply in judgment enforcement proceedings. (Code Civ. Proc., §708.130, subd. (b).) Moreover, discovery about assets in aid of enforcement of judgment does not touch upon confidential marital matters or communications. Therefore, the privilege afforded by Evidence Code section 980 does not apply either. (Troy v. Superior Court (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1006, 1013.) Nor is the information protected by Article I, section 1 of the California Constitution; the right to privacy does not extend as far as the moving parties contend. Mechanics Bank is not required to accept Mr. and Mrs. Kontrabecki's representations of fact as true, and may test those representations through discovery, including a debtor's examination and/or other discovery procedures. Mechanics Bank is entitled to conduct discovery into the nature and amount of the judgment debtors' assets, and that includes obtaining discovery from both Mr. and Mrs. Kontrabecki into the amount of those assets and whether they are in fact community property or separate property. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND CROWTHER VS. WALGREENS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 21, 2012, Line 5, DEFENDANT WALGREEN CO.'s, ERRONEOUSLY SUED HEREIN AS WALGREENS MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. The motion is denied. There exists a triable issue of material fact regarding whether Mr. Re is a managing agent that ratified the employees conduct. Mr. Re is the loss prevention manager for 38 of defendant's SF stores. (David Re Depo, 5:21-23; 23:3-6). Defendant's official shoplifting policy states "No employee is allowed to physically touch suspected shoplifters." Mr. Re reviewed the video of the arrest and did not think that the arrest was unreasonable or excessive. (Re Depo, 61:3-64:22). The employees violated defendant's no touch policy. (Re Depo, 56:2-8; 65:10-12). Mr. Re did not investigate the incident and to his knowledge, defendant never reprimanded the employees. (Re Depo, 88:14-19). Defendant's objections to the Re deposition (objections 8-13) are overruled. rulings.Case Title: AYUMI NAKAMOTO VS. KINFONG SIT, INDIVIDUALLY AND IN HIS CAPACITY AS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Order Compelling Answers To Deposition Questions, And Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, DECEMBER 21, 2012, LINE 6, DEFENDANT KINFONG SIT, INDIVIDUALLY AND IN HIS CAPACITY, Motion For Order Compelling Answers To Deposition Questions, And Production Of Documents. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's request for judicial notice is granted. Defendants' motion to compel answers at deposition and for production of documents is denied. Mr. Chan stated in his letter of November 9, 2012 that Defendants had agreed to continue Plaintiff's deposition "until further notice," and indicated that Plaintiff would not need to file her own motion for a protective order. Since there is currently no pending date or place for the deposition, the Court cannot compel any action by Plaintiff. The Court is seriously disturbed by Mr. Chan's failure to submit centrally important correspondence to the Court in connection with his motion, especially the November 9th letter regarding the indefinite continuance of the desired deposition. The Court regards this failure as an abuse of the discovery process and as a sign that the motion lacked substantial justification. Monetary sanctions are therefore awarded against Defendants' counsel in favor of Plaintiff in the amount of $2,750.00, to be paid within 20 days of the date of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CARLOS BEA et al VS. ENCOMPASS INSURANCE COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Encompass Insurance Company'S Motion To Compel Responses To First Set Of Request For Admission
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, DECEMBER 21, 2012, LINE 10, Defendant Encompass Insurance Company'S Motion To Compel Responses To First Set Of Request For Admission. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. Te Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted. Defendant cites to no authority requiring the service of a separate set of requests for admissions upon each Plaintiff. Also, there is nothing ambiguous or vague about Defendant's requests concerning "damages," as that term is defined in the requests and is commonly understood anyway. (Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1277, 1287.) Damages information is, of course, discoverable. (Hecht, Solberg, Robinson, Goldberg & Bagley v. Superior Court (2006) 137 Cal.App.4th 579, 589.) And even if Defendant was seeking admissions on damages purely for purposes of considering removal, it cannot be the function of this Court to determine whether the time for removal has passed, thus making that information irrelevant or non-discoverable. The determination of the validity of a removal is solely the province of the federal courts. (See Providence Health Plan v. McDowell (9th Cir. 2004) 368 F.3d 111168, 1171 [removal is an issue of federal subject matter jurisdiction]; 28 U.S.C. §1447, subd. (c) [remand motions to be determined by federal court].) Also, 28 U.S.C. §1446, subdivision (c)(3)(A) allows for discovery responses in state court to serve as a basis for removal in some instances, meaning that discovery responses may support an eventual removal petition. Furthermore, Defendant's service of the second sets of requests for admissions did not obviate the need for Plaintiffs to respond to the first set; if Plaintiffs have any objections to the second set, they should interpose them in the appropriate manner. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: QING LIU MO VS. LILLIAN MOCK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogs
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, DECEMBER 21, 2012, LINE 7, DEFENDANT LILLIAN MOCK, LU MOCK Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatorries. Pro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is denied insofar as it concerns the answers to requests for production, as those requests were omitted from Defendants' separate statement. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1345; Mills v. US Bank (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 871.) The motion is granted as to interrogatories nos. 1 through 6, 16, and 17 which are narrowly tailored and address specific allegations in the complaint. The motion is granted as to interrogatories nos. 13, 14, 15, and 25; they do not contain subparts or require reference to other materials. Plaintiff misreads Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1277; in fact, Clement expressly permits the interrogatories complained of here. (Clement, supra, 177 Cal.App.4th at 1290-91.) The motion is denied as to interrogatories nos. 7 and 19 as the supplemental responses cured any defects. The motion is also granted as to request for admission No. 14. Plaintiff is required to answer to the extent of her ability, and if there are facts readily ascertainable to her through ordinary inquiry, she must make such an inquiry and then answer insofar as she is able. (Code Civ. Proc., §2033.220, subd. (a).) If after such an inquiry is made Plaintiff truly lacks the knowledge to admit or deny this request, she must respond in compliance with section 2033.220, subd. (c). Plaintiff may not simply respond that responsive facts may be adduced at deposition. Additionally, there is nothing vague or ambiguous about the word "lied," so that objection is overruled. The request does not require the exposition of privileged information. Defendants are awarded monetary sanctions against Plaintiff and her counsel in the amount of $1,535.00, representing $1,475.00 in attorney fees and $60 filing fee, to be paid to Defendants' counsel within 20 days of the date of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LISA WALKER VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant James Meisenbach'S Hearing On Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Lisa Walker
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 21, 2012, Line 18, Defendant James Meisenbach'S Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Lisa Walker. To be heard at 9:00 am on the Discovery calendar. = (302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: LISA WALKER VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant James Meisenbach'S Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Lisa Walker
rulings.Rulings: Defendant James Meisenbach'S Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Lisa WalkerPro Tem Judge Jim Weixel, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion and sanctions are denied. CCP § 425.16(g) imposes a stay on discovery when a special motion to strike is filed. The Court can lift that stay and allow specified discovery to proceed as to the motion, but only if good cause exists. However, the overarching policy of the statute is to prevent a party from imposing ruinous costs on a defendant in an action subject to a special motion to strike. (Paterno v. Sup.Ct. (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1348-49; Sipple v. Foundation for Nat. Progress (1999) 71 Cal.App.4th 226, 247.) Accordingly, good cause for discovery will not be found to exist if the challenged pleading is legally deficient and there is no probability that the party opposing the special motion to strike can prevail. (Garment Workers Ctr. v. Sup.Ct. (2004) 117 Cal.App.4th 1156, 1162.) Taking the allegations of the Cross-Complaint at face value, it does not appear that Meisenbach has stated a legally tenable claim. First, as to the defamation claim, the allegedly defamatory statements were allegedly made in 2010, and Meisenbach had the City's investigative report by June 13, 2011. However, the Cross-Complaint was not filed until October 5, 2012. If these facts are true, that cause of action is time-barred. (§340(c).) Second, the facts in the Cross-Complaint, taken as true, indicate that Walker's alleged statements were made in the course of Walker's resort to administrative action with her public employer through its officials and as a public employee. If those facts are true, Walker's statements would be absolutely privileged and she would be immune from liability under any theory. (Civ. Code, § 47(b); Gov't Code, §821.6; Hansen v. Dep't. of Corr. & Rehab. (2008) 171 Cal.App.4th 1537, 1546.) Given these points, there is no need to analyze whether Meisenbach could obtain information through other means. Because Meisenbach has not stated a legally tenable claim in his Cross-Complaint, the Court will not lift the stay imposed by § 425.16(g). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to appeals@jimweixel.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.

rulings.Case Title: KARMA QUICK VS. JONATHAN FRIED et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Motion To Dismiss
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 21, 2012, Line 21, DEFENDANT JONATHAN FRIED'S Motion To Dismiss. The motion is denied. The Court treats this motion to dismiss as a demurrer per Barragan v. Banco BCH (1986) 188 Cal.App.3d 283, 299. The demurrer is overruled. The face of the complaint fails to demonstrate that plaintiff failed to exhaust an applicable administrative remedy. Indeed, the allegations at par. 7, which the Court takes as true, indicate otherwise. Plaintiff's request for sanctions/fees under CCP 128.5 is denied. That statute does not apply to this action because this action was commenced after December 31, 1994. See CCP 128.5(b)(1). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
